lectures.corpus1: Week 1 — Building a Korean–Chinese parallel corpus

questions.q1: What's the biggest difference between the digital yuan and Bitcoin?
questions.q2: Is the anonymity level of PBOC's CBDC equivalent to physical cash?
questions.q3: Could you explain how the hukou system limits migrant workers settling in cities, with concrete cases?
questions.q4: What are the main causes of hallucination in neural translation?
questions.q5: Could you contrast BLEU and chrF in parallel-corpus alignment?
questions.q6: Could I get a sneak peek of next semester's syllabus?
questions.q7: How does yuan digitisation affect Korean small business owners?
questions.q8: What is the scope of this exam?

answers.a1: The digital yuan (e-CNY) is a central-bank digital currency (CBDC) issued by the PBOC. Its issuer and trust model differ fundamentally from Bitcoin's decentralised proof-of-work ledger. e-CNY is distributed through a two-tier model (central bank → commercial banks → users) under PBOC backing.
answers.a2: As covered in lecture, e-CNY adopts a 'controllable anonymity' policy — small-value transactions are near-anonymous, but high-value transactions remain traceable. So it's not fully equivalent to cash.
answers.a3: The hukou system controls residence registration so migrant workers are excluded from urban welfare (healthcare, education) until they obtain urban hukou. Slides 7–9 cover the Beijing migrant-children education case.
answers.a4: Hallucination occurs when models produce plausible-but-fake outputs on inputs outside their training distribution. The main causes — exposure bias and the train/inference mismatch — are covered in week 4.
